Keywords: hypothesis, day one answer, evidence, testing, iterative, structured, strategic
Description: Form a Day One answer (initial hypothesis), decompose into testable sub-hypotheses, gather evidence to confirm or refute, then refine. Avoids boiling-the-ocean analysis by focusing on what matters.
When to Use: Complex strategic decisions with incomplete information where exhaustive analysis is impractical
Steps: 1. State your Day One answer (best current hypothesis). 2. Decompose into 3-5 testable sub-hypotheses. 3. For each sub-hypothesis, identify what evidence would prove/disprove it. 4. Gather minimum viable evidence. 5. Update hypothesis based on findings. 6. Repeat until confidence is sufficient.
Strengths: Prevents analysis paralysis; focuses effort on highest-value questions; naturally iterative
Limitations: Requires discipline to update hypothesis honestly; initial framing bias possible

Keywords: logic tree, MECE, options, exhaustive, decompose, map, alternatives, mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive
Description: Use MECE logic trees to map ALL options and sub-options exhaustively. Ensures no alternatives are missed before evaluation begins.
When to Use: Multi-option decisions where the full option space is unclear or risk of missing alternatives is high
Steps: 1. State the decision question clearly. 2. List top-level options (MECE). 3. For each option, decompose into sub-options. 4. Test completeness: are all possibilities covered? 5. Test exclusivity: do options overlap? 6. Prune obviously unviable branches. 7. Evaluate remaining options.
Strengths: Guarantees completeness; visual and structured; prevents premature narrowing
Limitations: Can be time-consuming for simple decisions; may generate too many options without clear prioritization

Keywords: weighted, criteria, matrix, scoring, comparison, evaluation, weights, transparent, auditable, score
Description: Define evaluation criteria, assign weights based on priorities, score each option 1-5 on each criterion, calculate weighted totals. Explicit, transparent, and auditable.
When to Use: Multi-option decisions with clear evaluation criteria where transparency and traceability matter
Steps: 1. List all options to evaluate. 2. Define 5-8 evaluation criteria. 3. Assign weights (must sum to 100). 4. Score each option 1-5 on each criterion. 5. Calculate weighted scores. 6. Compare totals and review sensitivity.
Strengths: Transparent and auditable; reduces emotional bias; easy to communicate; supports group alignment
Limitations: Requires agreement on criteria and weights upfront; can create false precision; garbage-in-garbage-out

Keywords: sensitivity, assumptions, variables, uncertainty, flip, robustness, tornado, what-if
Description: Test which assumptions or criteria changes would flip the decision. Identifies what matters most and what is noise. Reveals decision robustness.
When to Use: Decisions where confidence in key inputs is uncertain and you need to know which assumptions actually matter
Steps: 1. Identify the current leading option. 2. List all key assumptions and inputs. 3. For each assumption, vary it across plausible range. 4. Record which variations change the decision. 5. Rank assumptions by decision impact. 6. Focus additional analysis only on decision-sensitive assumptions.
Strengths: Reveals what actually drives the decision; prevents over-analysis of non-sensitive factors; builds confidence
Limitations: Requires a quantifiable decision model; may miss interaction effects between variables

Keywords: expected value, probability, outcomes, rational, uncertainty, calculation, quantitative, EV
Description: Assign probabilities to outcomes, multiply by value, sum for each option. The rational choice under uncertainty when outcomes and probabilities can be estimated.
When to Use: Decisions with quantifiable outcomes and estimable probabilities where rational optimization is appropriate
Steps: 1. List all possible outcomes for each option. 2. Estimate probability of each outcome. 3. Estimate value (monetary or utility) of each outcome. 4. Multiply probability x value for each. 5. Sum expected values per option. 6. Choose highest EV (adjusted for risk tolerance).
Strengths: Mathematically rigorous; handles uncertainty explicitly; comparable across options
Limitations: Requires probability and value estimates that may be unreliable; ignores risk preference; poor for one-shot decisions

Keywords: scenario, planning, uncertainty, futures, robustness, external, environment, strategic, ambiguity
Description: Define 2-3 key external uncertainties, build 4-9 scenarios from combinations, evaluate each option across all scenarios. Identifies robust choices that perform well regardless of future.
When to Use: Strategic decisions with high external uncertainty where the future environment is genuinely unknowable
Steps: 1. Identify 2-3 key external uncertainties (not in your control). 2. Define 2-3 plausible values for each. 3. Build scenario matrix (combinations). 4. Name each scenario and describe it. 5. Evaluate each option under each scenario. 6. Select option that is robust across scenarios or identify hedging strategies.
Strengths: Handles deep uncertainty; prevents overcommitment to single forecast; builds organizational preparedness
Limitations: Can generate too many scenarios; subjective scenario selection; does not assign probabilities

Keywords: pros, cons, fixes, advantages, disadvantages, mitigate, quick, simple, binary, tradeoff
Description: List pros and cons for each option, then for each con ask 'can it be fixed or mitigated?' Transforms binary thinking into nuanced evaluation by exploring whether downsides are addressable.
When to Use: Binary or few-option decisions needing quick assessment where a simple structured approach is sufficient
Steps: 1. List all pros for each option. 2. List all cons for each option. 3. For each con, ask: can this be fixed, mitigated, or is it permanent? 4. Mark fixable cons differently from permanent ones. 5. Re-evaluate: does the option look different once fixable cons are addressed? 6. Decide based on remaining permanent trade-offs.
Strengths: Simple and fast; accessible to all stakeholders; reveals that many cons are solvable; reduces fear-based rejection
Limitations: Not rigorous for complex multi-option decisions; no weighting mechanism; subjective assessment of fixability

Keywords: pre-mortem, failure, risk, blind spots, stress test, retrospective, prospective hindsight, assume failure
Description: Assume the decision has already failed catastrophically. Work backward to identify what went wrong. Surfaces blind spots and risks before committing.
When to Use: High-stakes irreversible decisions where undetected risks could be catastrophic
Steps: 1. State the proposed decision clearly. 2. Imagine it is 12 months later and the decision failed spectacularly. 3. Each participant independently writes what went wrong. 4. Share and compile all failure modes. 5. Categorize by likelihood and severity. 6. For high-risk failure modes, develop mitigation plans or reconsider the decision.
Strengths: Surfaces hidden risks; overcomes optimism bias; psychologically safe way to critique; works well in groups
Limitations: Can be demoralizing if overused; may generate unlikely risks; requires honest participation

Keywords: reversibility, one-way door, two-way door, speed, iterate, meta, decision speed, reversible, irreversible
Description: Classify the decision as a one-way door (irreversible, high stakes) or two-way door (reversible, can iterate). Two-way doors should be decided quickly; one-way doors deserve deep analysis.
When to Use: Any decision — as the first step to determine how much analysis effort is warranted
Steps: 1. Ask: If this decision turns out wrong, can we reverse or significantly change it? 2. If YES (two-way door): decide quickly, set a review date, iterate. 3. If NO (one-way door): invest in thorough analysis, use other frameworks. 4. If PARTIALLY reversible: identify which aspects are reversible and which are not. 5. Apply proportional analytical effort.
Strengths: Prevents over-analysis of reversible decisions; ensures proper investment in irreversible ones; meta-framework that improves decision speed
Limitations: Oversimplifies — most decisions are partially reversible; binary classification may be too coarse

Keywords: iterative, hypothesis, testing, bayesian, evidence, update, learn, experiment, minimum viable, data-poor
Description: Start with your best guess, design a minimum viable test, update based on evidence, repeat. Bayesian approach that learns from small experiments before full commitment.
When to Use: Data-poor environments where learning is possible before full commitment, and iteration is feasible
Steps: 1. State initial hypothesis (best guess with current information). 2. Identify the cheapest way to test it (minimum viable experiment). 3. Define what evidence would confirm or refute. 4. Run the test. 5. Update hypothesis based on results (Bayesian update). 6. Repeat until confidence is sufficient or deadline requires decision.
Strengths: Minimizes cost of learning; naturally self-correcting; works with incomplete data; reduces commitment risk
Limitations: Requires ability to test before committing; slower for time-pressured decisions; may never reach full confidence
